front: What is the fundamental operation and worst-case time complexity of the Selection Sort algorithm?
back: Selection Sort repeatedly finds the minimum element from the unsorted portion and swaps it with the element at the current position. Its worst-case and average-case time complexity is O(n²), where n is the number of items.

front: How does Bubble Sort work, and what is a key characteristic of its operation?
back: Bubble Sort works by repeatedly stepping through the list, comparing adjacent elements and swapping them if they are in the wrong order. This process is repeated until the list is sorted. A key characteristic is that after each complete pass, the largest unsorted element 'bubbles up' to its correct position at the end of the list.

front: What is the main advantage of Selection Sort over Bubble Sort?
back: The main advantage is that Selection Sort makes a lower number of swaps. It performs only O(n) swaps in the worst case, whereas Bubble Sort can perform O(n²) swaps. This can be significant if the cost of swapping items is high.

front: Are Selection Sort and Bubble Sort stable sorting algorithms?
back: No, the standard implementation of Selection Sort is not stable because swaps can change the relative order of equal elements. The standard implementation of Bubble Sort is stable because it only swaps adjacent elements when they are out of order, preserving the relative order of equal items.

front: What is the best-case time complexity for Bubble Sort, and under what condition is it achieved?
back: The best-case time complexity for Bubble Sort is O(n). This occurs when the algorithm is optimized with a flag to check if any swaps were made in a pass, and the input list is already sorted. The algorithm would then make a single pass and terminate early.
